current_fact.fact_text: On March 10, 1966, South Vietnam’s Prime Minister Nguyễn Cao Kỳ dismissed his popular rival General Nguyễn Chánh Thi, igniting protests and deep fissures inside a country already at war. The power struggle exposed how fragile the political scaffolding behind the frontline really was.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

current_fact.match_reason: A story of internal dissent and taking power from entrenched generals pairs with an episode celebrating those who 'refuse to ask for permission' in another arena: rock.

queue.fact_text: On March 10, 1990, Haitian leader Prosper Avril was forced from power after only 18 months of rule, following his own coup in 1988. The swift reversal underlined how fragile military regimes can be when they rule without deep public legitimacy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

queue.match_reason: A Caribbean nation’s unstable leadership sits alongside an episode tracing another Caribbean-rooted force: urban music rising from the streets rather than from palaces.

queue.fact_text: On March 10, 2000, the dot-com bubble reached its giddy peak as the NASDAQ Composite closed at 5,048.62. Many investors believed the internet had abolished old economic rules—just before reality reasserted itself and trillions in paper wealth evaporated.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: The peak of internet-fueled financial euphoria pairs with an episode reflecting on our tangled, sometimes unhealthy relationship with always-online technology.

queue.fact_text: On March 10, 2006’s future echoes, March 10, 2023 saw Silicon Valley Bank collapse in a rapid bank run, becoming the second-largest bank failure in U.S. history. In just 48 hours, whispers turned into mass withdrawals, revealing how fragile confidence can be in a hyperconnected financial system.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A dramatic institutional failure invites the kind of reflection on mistakes, adaptation, and long-term learning that this episode gently explores.

queue.fact_text: On March 10, 2017, South Korea’s Constitutional Court unanimously upheld the impeachment of President Park Geun-hye, removing her from office over a sweeping corruption scandal. Millions of citizens had protested peacefully in candlelight vigils, turning public squares into luminous demands for accountability.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: A society breaking out of corrupt patterns through peaceful protest connects with an episode about unboxing, confronting uncomfortable truths, and redefining identity.

queue.fact_text: On March 10 in the year 298, Roman Emperor Maximian concluded a campaign in North Africa and entered Carthage in triumph. The celebration marked Rome’s momentary dominance over a region that had once produced one of its greatest rivals, Hannibal’s Carthage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: Roman triumph in North Africa and modern Italian voices in the mountains both trace how Mediterranean power and culture radiate across landscapes and centuries.

queue.fact_text: On March 10, 1814’s geopolitical echoes aside, another thread of history on this date is how often the map itself has shifted—by war, treaty, or accident. From Maximian’s Africa to the Anglo-Siamese border, every line on today’s globes hides a story of negotiation, resistance, or chance.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: An episode about journeys and territories in sound pairs with a meta-fact about shifting borders and sovereignty behind multiple March 10 events.

queue.fact_text: Another quiet thread on March 10 is how often unseen systems—rings of ice, radio waves, bank balances, tectonic faults—suddenly reveal their power. Whether it’s a star winking behind Uranus’s rings or a bank run unfolding on a screen, much of reality moves in layers most of us rarely notice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: An exploration of hidden, atmospheric systems fits the layered, subterranean feel of trip-hop and its cinematic, behind-the-scenes textures.

queue.fact_text: On March 10, across centuries, communication keeps reinventing itself—from the first telephone call to wartime propaganda broadcasts to modern smartphones glowing in our hands. Each leap reshapes how human stories travel: who gets heard, who gets silenced, and how fast a rumor can circle the planet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: An episode focused on a technology that reshaped vocal communication in music echoes the broader evolution of how voices are carried and altered over time.

queue.fact_text: Look across all the March 10 stories—earthquakes, coups, discoveries, impeachments—and a pattern appears: transformation often arrives suddenly, then quietly reshapes everyday life for decades. What feels like a single day in the news becomes a fault line in the long, slow music of history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A reflective episode about healing and transitions is a fitting companion for a meta-fact that looks back at March 10 as a day when turning points quietly accumulate.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1982, a rare astronomical syzygy occurred when all nine then-recognized planets from Mercury to Pluto lined up on the same side of the Sun. Despite apocalyptic predictions in popular media, Earth felt no catastrophic effects—only a reminder of how small we are in a precisely balanced celestial dance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: An episode about reimagining 'space' pairs naturally with a planetary alignment that turned cosmic emptiness into a grand geometric pattern.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 2006, NASA's Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter slid into orbit around Mars after a delicate 27-minute braking burn. Since then, it has quietly become one of humanity’s greatest interplanetary photographers, mapping the Red Planet in exquisite detail and scouting landing sites for future missions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A mission that settles into orbit around another world fits the calm, orbital mood of an episode literally titled 'Slow Planet'.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1977, astronomers unexpectedly discovered that Uranus is encircled by a system of dark, narrow rings. The finding came not from an image, but from tiny flickers of starlight as the planet and its hidden rings passed in front of a distant star.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: Mystery in the outer Solar System meets a set that opens in cosmic ambience and 'solar wind' textures before circling outward like planetary rings.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1535, Bishop Tomás de Berlanga literally drifted into history when ocean currents blew his ship off course and into the volcanic Galápagos Islands. His accidental landfall foreshadowed a place that would later reshape biology through Darwin’s theory of evolution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: An accidental arrival at a harsh, isolated archipelago resonates with desert music shaped by survival in remote, elemental landscapes.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1496, after founding Santo Domingo—one of the oldest continuously inhabited European-founded cities in the Americas—Christopher Columbus sailed back to Spain, leaving his brother in charge. The settlement became a springboard for further exploration and a crucible of cultural collision.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A seafaring voyage that seeded a new city mirrors an episode built around boat travel, coasts, and the cultural tides of old-world seas.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1876, Alexander Graham Bell made the first successful telephone call, summoning his assistant with the words, “Mr. Watson, come here, I want to see you.” With that scratchy sentence, distance became something voices could bend.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: The birth of the telephone and an episode about our phone-obsessed era bookend the story of how human connection moved into wires and screens.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1873, audiences in Baku watched the first Azerbaijani play, “The Adventures of the Vizier of the Khan of Lenkaran,” bringing local language and satire to the stage. It marked a turning point where theatre became a tool for cultural reflection in the Caucasus.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: Both the Azerbaijani play and flamenco's evolution show how performance arts become vessels for regional identity and bold new storytelling.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1909, the Anglo-Siamese Treaty redrew borders in Southeast Asia, as Siam (Thailand) ceded control of several Malay states to Britain. The lines drafted on that day still shape the map and identities of southern Thailand and northern Malaysia.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A treaty that reshaped Thailand’s borders fits with an episode recorded in Thailand, steeped in the country’s contemporary cultural rhythms.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1933, the Long Beach earthquake rocked Southern California, collapsing hundreds of school buildings and killing around 108 people. The tragedy sparked strict new building codes that quietly saved countless lives in later quakes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden seismic jolt reshaping a city parallels a high-voltage rock set that hits like a controlled, cathartic 'electric shock'.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1945, U.S. bombers unleashed a firebombing raid on Tokyo that created a storm of flames, killing over 100,000 people in a single night. The city’s wooden neighborhoods turned into a sea of fire, a lesser-discussed but devastating chapter of World War II.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: An episode about storms, endurance, and emerging from dark chapters offers a contemplative frame for one of history’s most destructive air raids.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1949, Mildred Gillars—better known as “Axis Sally”—was convicted of treason for broadcasting Nazi propaganda to American troops during World War II. She became a haunting symbol of how radio, a medium of comfort and music, could be twisted into a weapon.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A story of radio used for propaganda is counterbalanced by a politically charged episode centered on music that challenges power instead of serving it.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1944, amid Nazi occupation, the Greek resistance established the Political Committee of National Liberation as a rival government in the mountains. It printed its own banknotes, ran courts, and demonstrated how guerrilla movements can briefly build parallel states.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A resistance government born in the mountains reflects a radical shift in perspective, echoing an episode about changing viewpoints and stepping into new spaces.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1814, Napoleon Bonaparte suffered a significant defeat at the Battle of Laon, where outnumbered French forces were pushed back by the Allied armies. It was one of the blows that chipped away at his aura of invincibility and led toward his first abdication.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

published.match_reason: An emperor losing his invincible 'star' status aligns with an episode about evolving personas, fame, and learning to live beyond raw power.

published.fact_text: On March 10, 1952, Fulgencio Batista seized power in Cuba through a military coup, canceling elections and installing a dictatorship that would later provoke the Cuban Revolution. His rule turned the island into a playground for foreign interests and a tinderbox of local resistance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A Caribbean coup and the roots of Latin American political tension resonate with an episode centered on Residente’s sharp, revolutionary Latin voice.

The power struggle exposed how fragile the political scaffolding behind the frontline really was.
